chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the Committee on Governmental Operations hearing, reminds everyone to silence devices, and instructs those wishing to testify to fill out testimony slips.
chapters.title: Staff housekeeping and meeting opening

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, ladies and gentlemen.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to the New York City Council hearing for the Committee on Governmental Operations, State and Federal Legislation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, I'd like to remind everyone to please silence all devices and at no point during the hearing may you approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to testify at today's hearing, please see a sergeant at the back of the room to fill out a testimony slip, even if you have already registered previously online.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Brewer introduces herself as chair, thanks attendees, and describes community boards as the most local form of government designed to give neighborhood residents a voice in governing and feedback to agencies.
chapters.title: Chair Brewer opening on community boards as local government

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I am Gail Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I am the chair of the governmental operations, state and federal legislation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank everyone who's here.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know that there are those who are going to be speaking from campaign finance, federal president's office, civic engagement commission, conflicts of interest board, as well as members of the public who are very interested in community boards and other topics.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We're going to be talking about community boards.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think you know they're the most local form of government.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I certainly know that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They are intentionally designed to give people who live or work in the neighborhood a voice in governing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They provide valuable feedback to agencies and other government bodies on how services are being delivered.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They also provide local policymakers with information on how their decisions could affect a local community district.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think I know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think I've been to more community board members than any other human being I know.

chapters.summary: Brewer explains that borough presidents appoint all community board members, half from council member nominations, and that the 2018 ballot proposal required seeking diverse applicants. She expresses support for keeping council members out of direct appointments.
chapters.title: Brewer on community board appointment process

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Borough presidents, and I was one of them, appoint all community board members.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Half of the members must come from nominations made by the local council member or members.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Each borough president has their own method for selecting members, but generally applicants must fill out an online application.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And appear for an interview by a representative from the borough president's office.

chapters.summary: Brewer criticizes the 2018 ballot proposal imposing four-term limits on community board members, calling it a terrible idea that will cause a brain drain of experienced members, particularly on complex land use issues, by 2027.
chapters.title: Brewer on 2018 term limits and brain drain concern

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: A 2018 ballot proposal imposed a requirement that borough presidents seek out community board applications from diverse backgrounds.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Council members do not directly appoint members, which I think is a good thing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They can only nominate members who then may or may not be appointed by the borough president.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Actually, I think that's how it should stay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Community board members are all volunteers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They are unpaid.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They're expected to attend monthly board meetings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I used to take people off who did not do that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In addition to serving at least one committee, most of them meet monthly.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Board members serve two-year terms.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The 2018 ballot proposal also imposed term limits on board members.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I thought that was a terrible idea.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No members may serve more than four consecutive terms.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The first members subject to these new terms will need to be replaced by 2020-17, which is a brain drain.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: While this will create room for newer members, it will also mean the loss of long-time members.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: members with significant knowledge about complex issues, particularly land use.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We want to make sure that the community boards themselves, along with borough presidents and the Civic Engagement Commission, Benjamin knows how I feel about that, are all prepared for this unprecedented turnover.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I did vote against that in the charter too, just so you know.

chapters.summary: Brewer describes district managers as full-time paid employees responsible for daily operations, complaints, and meetings. She notes the Civic Engagement Commission was created in 2018 to assist community boards, primarily through Zoom trainings.
chapters.title: Brewer on district managers and CEC role

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: District managers who are full-time paid employees are responsible for the day-to-day operations of the community boards.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Their duties include processing complaints, presiding over the district service cabinet, and other duties assigned by the board.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: District managers are responsible for organizing both committee and full board meetings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The Civic Engagement Commission created by the 2018 Charter Revision Commission was formed to provide assistance to community boards.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That assistance seemed to come primarily in the form of Zoom trainings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Designed for community board members with some additional trainings that appear to be designed for the board's executive leadership.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There are past trainings that are available on the CEC's website that have included parliamentary proceedings, the budget, land use and equity planning.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: As the board president, just so you know, I did all that, just so you know.

chapters.summary: Brewer outlines the five pieces of legislation: Intro 12A on AI disclosure in elections, Intro 320 on Staten Island flag, Intro 322 on council member community board appointments, Intro 501A on district manager terms, and Intro 951 on prediction market insider trading. She expresses personal opinions on several.
chapters.title: Brewer introduces five bills on agenda

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In addition to doing oversight, we will also be hearing five pieces of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We'll be hearing proposed intro number 12A, sponsored by the speaker, which we were

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: require the disclosure of digitally altered content in local elections.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Also, Intro 320, sponsored by Councilmember Murano, which would codify the official flag of Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think that's a good idea.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Introduction 322, sponsored by Councilmember Murano, which would give council members the power to make appointments to community boards.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know how I feel about that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Intro 501A, sponsored by Councilmember Williams, which will alter the terms of employment for district managers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't like that one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And Intro 951, sponsored by Councilmember Thomas Henry, which will prevent policymakers and their relatives from trading on prediction markets using insider information.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer thanks committee staff Jahari Frazier, Erica Cohen, and Simul Hamid, then calls on Council Member Frank Morano to speak first since he needs to leave for another meeting.
chapters.title: Brewer thanks staff and calls Morano

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'd like to thank the following staff from the Government Operations Committee, Jahari Frazier, Erica Cohen, and Simul Hamid, and everyone working in the background.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I would like to first call on Frank Morano, who is also the chair of the Veterans Committee.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And he needs to go.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So he should start first in terms of his comments.

chapters.summary: Morano presents his bill to codify the Staten Island flag, noting only Brooklyn and the Bronx have official flags in law. He says the flag celebrates borough identity and makes Staten Island feel part of the city, and introduces designer Amal Micah as Staten Island's Betsy Ross.
chapters.title: Morano on Intro 320 - Staten Island flag

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chair, for the opportunity to speak.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And as much as I would love to stay and devour every minute of today's testimony, I am chairing a meeting that began 10 minutes ago.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I'm going to run next door and then hopefully come back if this meeting goes on a bit.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I appreciate the chair's generous introduction.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I also appreciate the.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: open-mindedness with which you're approaching all the pieces of legislation today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I certainly concur that there is not a human being alive that has attended more community board meetings than Gail Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I do want to say a bit about Intro 320, which is my bill,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: which is sponsored by the entirety of the city council delegation from Staten Island and a good portion of the chunk of the city council as a whole, which would give Staten Island an official flag.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Currently, while every borough has at least an unofficial flag, there are only two boroughs that have official flags codified in the law.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Those are Brooklyn and the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The Staten Island flag, which has been our unofficial flag since 2019, and we're really honored to have the director of visual design from Staten Island Borough President's Office, Amal Micah, here

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: to speak about how this flag was created, some of the incredible imagery, some of the symbolism, some of the history behind the flag.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: When I've been talking about this bill for over a year, the resounding complaint against it, really the only one against it, is don't you have anything else better to do?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And by the way, that's a complaint from everybody about anything.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But the answer really is no.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I'll tell you why.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This bill is so meaningful to Staten Island and to me.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: one swoop, namely adopting and giving us a flag, this city council has the ability to do two things.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One, celebrate, recognize borough identity, and two, makes Staten Island feel like we're actually a part of this city.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I can tell you, both of those things from time to time are in short supply.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And we'll get into why, and I'm eager to hear Amal Micah, Staten Island's Betsy Ross, in his testimony a bit later.

chapters.summary: Morano argues his bill would make council member nominations binding rather than advisory, citing borough presidents who ignore nominations and leave candidates in limbo. He says the bill would let voters decide the appointment process through a ballot question.
chapters.title: Morano on Intro 322 - council member community board appointments

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Then, intro 322, my bill on the community board appointment process.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This is basically just codifying what I believe was always intended to be

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: the law, where instead of council members recommending half of the community board members, council members would get to pick and nominate in a binding manner half of the community board members.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Why is this important?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Well, I think it's important for a few reasons.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One, on a practical level, we've seen borough presidents in multiple boroughs, not just mine, simply refuse to act on community board nominees from council members.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The people, in many cases dedicated community activists that are nominated by the council members, are never told that they're accepted.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They're never told that they're rejected.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They just stay in this perpetual limbo.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Additionally, by ignoring nominations from local council members, these are often the people that know the individual neighborhoods best.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And adding people from these neighborhoods that understand the unique concerns that they have, rather than simply allow the only binding appointment to be a borough-wide voice,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: it adds to a diversity, not just when it comes to gender, race, age, or anything like that, but a diversity of experience, a diversity of worldview, which is sorely lacking now.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And the best thing about this bill, if the council adopts it, is all it does is give the voters the right to choose for themselves whether council members should have this appointment process.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And to me, that's the best situation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Put it on the ballot.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let those of us that think council members should have binding appointment make our argument.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let those that think that the borough president should get to appoint everybody make their argument and let the voters decide.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I'm very excited about both bills being heard today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'm grateful that dozens of my colleagues in the council in both parties in all five boroughs have sponsored both bills.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I'm eager to see them voted on by this body and by the council as a whole.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Brewer expresses concern that council members lack staff to vet community board nominees properly, unlike borough presidents who have established processes. She worries about unvetted political appointments and suggests mandating borough presidents to have a process instead.
chapters.title: Brewer responds to Morano on vetting concerns for Intro 322

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to talk about your second bill to point out that the borough presidents, if they're good, and they're not all, have a process that says this person is qualified, this person isn't.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I worry that I, Gail Brewer, if given the opportunity, would not have the ability to put that person through a process because I don't have the staff to do it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what my concern is, so that they're not vetted.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So that's why I have a concern.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Now, the borough president has no process.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's a different story.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Maybe they should be mandated to have one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I am concerned about people who are not vetted and who are politically interesting to the council member.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That could happen to me, and I want that not to happen to anybody.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So that's where my concern is.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I have great respect for you, sir.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I will tell you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Councilmember Thomas Henry to give a statement on your bill in 2951.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shanel Thomas-Henry presents her bill to prohibit elected officials, policymakers, and their immediate family members from using non-public government information to profit through prediction markets and event-based trading, framing it as an ethics and trust issue.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry on Intro 951 - prediction market insider trading

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Good morning and thank you, Chair Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you for holding today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: We often think of insider information in the context of the stock market.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: But as technology evolves, so do the ways that privileged information can be used for personal gain.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Prediction markets and event-based trading platforms have created a new frontier where individuals can place financial wagers on the outcome of elections,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: policy decisions, regulatory actions, and other events that may be influenced by government activity.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: The question before us today is straightforward.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Should public officials or policymakers be allowed to profit from information that the public does not have access to?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I believe the answer is no.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Public service is built on trust.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: New Yorkers expect that decisions made by elected officials and government policymakers are guided by the public interest, not by personal financial opportunities tied to non-public information.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: That's why we're here today.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Intro 951 is about making sure our ethics laws keep pace with the times.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: This bill will prohibit elected officials, policymakers, and their immediate family members from using non-public government information to profit through prediction markets and event-based trading.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Because public service should never come with a private advantage.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: New Yorkers trust us with information, responsibilities, and decisions that impact their lives every day.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: They deserve to know that people serving them are not also looking for opportunities to personally profit from information that the public does not have.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And while prediction markets may be new, the principle behind this legislation is not.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: We already understand that insider trading undermines trust.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: We already understand that public service carries a responsibility to avoid even the appearance of self-dealing.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: This bill applies those same basic values to a rapidly emerging marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: At a time when trust in government is fragile, we should be doing everything we can to strengthen it.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: That means being proactive.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It means anticipating challenges before they become problems.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And it means ensuring that our laws reflect not just what is legal, but what New Yorkers expect from those who serve them.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: This legislation is about fairness, it's about transparency, and it's about protecting the integrity of public service.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I want to thank Chair Brewer and the members of the Committee on Governmental Operations for hearing this issue today, and I look forward from here.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: hearing from all the witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We've been joined by Council Members Wang and Council Member Wong.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then I'm going to read, this is ironic, a statement from Council Member Dr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Natasha Williams.

chapters.summary: Brewer reads a statement from Council Member Natasha Williams supporting Intro 501A, which would establish four-year terms for district managers with reappointment and clarify circumstances for removal by borough presidents, including failure to attend meetings or meet reporting obligations.
chapters.title: Brewer reads Williams statement on Intro 501A - district manager terms

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And it says to the committee, and it's about interim 501, it says, this is her talking, not me, just so you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate the opportunity to submit this statement in support of Intro 501A.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Community boards play a vital role in New York City's local governance.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They serve as an important bridge between residents, city agencies, borough presidents, and elected officials, helping to ensure that community voices are reflected in local decision-making.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: At the center of each community board's operations is the district manager.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: District managers are often the first point of contact for residents seeking assistance with quality of life concerns, service complaints, and neighborhood issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They help coordinate responses from city agencies, support board operations, and play a critical role in ensuring that community boards function effectively.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Given the importance of this role, it is essential that we establish clear expectations and accountability measures regarding the terms of employment for district managers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 501A would establish four-year terms for district managers while allowing community boards to reappoint individuals for successive terms.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The bill also clarifies the circumstances under which a borough president may remove a district manager.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: prior to the end of their term, including repeated failure to attend full board meetings or failure to ensure required reporting obligations are met.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This legislation is intended to provide greater structure, consistency, and accountability while preserving the ability of community boards to retain effective leadership.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Clear standards help strengthen institutional stability and ensure district managers remain responsive to both their boards and the communities they serve.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Strong community boards require strong leadership and strong systems of accountability.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This intro helps advance both.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's from Dr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Councilmember Natasha Williams.

chapters.summary: Carolyn Miller of the Conflicts of Interest Board testifies that the proposed legislation regulates conduct already prohibited by existing conflicts of interest law, and that the annual disclosure changes would create a dramatic expansion of reporting requirements impacting people beyond those covered by the bill.
chapters.title: COIB testimony - Carolyn on existing law coverage of prediction markets

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And now we're going to call the first speaker, Ethan Carrier from the New York City Conflicts of Interest Board, also known as COIB.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And also Carolyn Miller, also from COIB.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Begin whenever you wish.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I swear.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Oh, you have to swear.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Swear you win, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's yet to be sworn in.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I always forget that part.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We will now hear testimony from the Conflicts of Interest Board.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Before we begin, I will administer the oath of affirmation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Panelists, please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Yes, we certainly appreciate the importance of public servants not trading on government information or using their city positions to violate the public trust for their own financial benefit.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The legislation that's proposed

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: regulates conduct that is already prohibited by the conflicts of interest board.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The legislation has two components to it.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The first of which, and Ethan Carrier, our board's general counsel, will speak more in depth, addresses conduct that's already prohibited by the conflicts of interest law.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And the second component changes to the city's

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: annual disclosure law would reflect a dramatic expansion of the reporting requirements that reflects nothing comparable currently in the annual disclosure law and create a burden on people way beyond those who are regulated by this conduct.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Substantial policymakers reflect just a fraction of people who file annual disclosure reports, and the reporting that's proposed in this legislation would impact people way beyond those that are arguably covered by the proposed legislation.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So let's first discuss how this law is already addressed in the current conflicts of interest law.

chapters.summary: Ethan Carrier explains that the City Charter already broadly prohibits public servants from disclosing or using confidential city information for private gain, covering prediction market scenarios. He notes the misuse of position restriction would cover any remaining gaps involving non-public information.
chapters.title: COIB testimony - Ethan Carrier on confidential information restrictions

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: This is an incredibly important subject.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: I mean, this is a real risk and something that needs to be addressed.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We believe that the law that already exists does address it.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: First, the city charter has a remarkably robust

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: provision prohibiting the disclosure or the use of confidential city information by public servants.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: And so I'm just going to talk about those two sections of that charter prohibition.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: The first part is the prohibition against public servants disclosing

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: confidential city information and this is a remarkably broad prohibition it really prohibits public servants from disclosing confidential city information for any purpose regardless of whether there is any

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: any benefit or loss or anything else.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: It doesn't matter who they're disclosing the information to.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: The mere disclosure all by itself is a violation of the city charter.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: So a public servant who disclosed confidential city information for the purpose of

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: for their own purpose or somebody else's purpose related to making money on a prediction market would certainly be encompassed by that restriction.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Again, regardless of to whom the disclosure is made.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: The second part of the confidential information restriction is a prohibition against public servants using confidential information.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: For their own private gain or for the private gain of associated parties.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: And associated parties is a certain set of close family members and also people with whom they have a business or financial relationship.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: So a business partner, a roommate, someone to whom they owe money, these are all people whose interests essentially function as their own interests under the conflicts of interest law.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: They could not use confidential city information to obtain any kind of benefit, again, for themselves through a prediction market or for one of these associated family members or business or financial related parties.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: To the extent that those two provisions don't cover a public servant's actions related to a prediction market, we think

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: that the city charter's prohibition against the use of the authority of their city office to obtain a private gain, again, for themselves or an associated party would cover the rest of it.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: That is, if you used your city position to obtain the confidential information that you would use, this would be a violation of the...

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Use of the prohibition against using your city position or the law as written

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: has uses a definition I'm saying confidential information because that's an established definition within the conflicts of interest law this law uses the term non-public information which is

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: maybe broader and we're a little concerned.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We don't know exactly what the scope of that might be, but if a public servant were to use information

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: that they obtained that was non-public but not confidential, the misuse of position restriction would likely prohibit most, if not all, of the conduct related to obtaining or using that information.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: For their own private gain or the private gain of those associated parties that I've just described.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: So to the extent that the confidential information restrictions don't cover it, we think the misuse of position restriction would cover it.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: So again, we think this is an incredibly important

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: new area that needs to be thought about.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We think

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: the law as it exists covers this concern and we believe the board would robustly enforce this provision if a public servant were obtaining a private gain

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: in any way related to this or disclosing confidential information even again without a private gain for themselves or anyone else.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: I'll hand it back to Carolyn to talk about the public disclosure piece of this.

chapters.summary: Carolyn argues the bill's requirement to report all prediction market transactions of $50 or more is unprecedented, with nothing comparable in current disclosure law. She notes it would impact candidates for office who are not public servants and would be the first reporting of losses.
chapters.title: COIB testimony - Carolyn on annual disclosure expansion concerns

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So the second piece of the legislation talks about a major change to the annual disclosure law where it requires the reporting of all transactions.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Of $50 or more related to event contracts on prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: There's nothing, absolutely nothing even comparable in the current annual disclosure law.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: For example, the closest thing would be the reporting of securities.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And a current public servant can buy and sell securities over the course of the reporting year.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: There's no reporting.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Of the purchase or sale of any security, of who you bought it from, who you sold it to, when you made that transaction.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The only reporting requirement currently is the assets that you hold on the final day of the reporting period.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Similarly, there's no requirement of reporting of any other kinds of gambling.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: You don't have to report

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: When you placed a bet, what casino you placed it at, what the bet was for, what you earned.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: There's nothing comparable in the annual disclosure law.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: You just have to report your winning.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: on the final day of the reporting period.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And there's no reporting anywhere in the annual disclosure law of losses.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And this would be the first time that there would be any reporting of losses.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So this would be a dramatic expansion of any kind of comparable reporting in the annual disclosure law.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: I'd also note that the...

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The regulatory regime, the conduct, is directed at substantial policymakers.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And substantial policymakers may be comprised about a third, if not less, of people who report under the annual disclosure law.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: For example, there's

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: every candidate for elective office in the city has to file an annual disclosure law, this provision would apply to those people.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Those people, many of whom are not public servants at the time they run for office, so they're having to report conduct which is totally fine, not prohibited, maybe thus deterring them from

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: seeking public office because they'd have to report conduct which is in no way prohibited, would it reflect a conflict of interest, or anything like that.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: This, so there's, I think, a lot of ways in which this is maybe more than what is necessary given the current state.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: It is a brand new field.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Prediction markets, why this particular robust reporting regime where there isn't anything similar seems to be disproportionate to the concern.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And we're happy to take any questions on either of these.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Councilmember, do you want to go ahead with questions?

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks what additions are needed given the rapidly changing environment. Carolyn responds that education is more effective than annual disclosure for preventing violations, since disclosure reports cover conduct from nearly a year prior.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions COIB on education and prevention

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So I have a couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: You mentioned that this may be more than what is necessary.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So given that this rapid changing environment, has your team looked at what would be

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: needed or should be added to make sure that

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: we deal with this?

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: I think generally the annual, well, so there's like always two things.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The first thing is first education, because the goal is not to sort of catch people after the fact, but to prevent violations before they occur.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So our focus is always on public servants to try to educate them about their obligations under the conflicts of interest law and to protect confidential city information.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And we do that all the time.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: We're very rigorous in trying to reach out to public servants in every way we possibly can about protecting confidential city information.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And there's all kinds of confidential city information that one might not place an event contract on.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Confidential information about someone's public assistance benefits, about child abuse and neglect investigations, things that public servants are incredibly private that

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: we have for decades ensured that that information is kept confidential.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The annual disclosure law in particular is sort of a very difficult way to regulate that conduct because it has to do with

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: things that happened almost a full year.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So, right, the council has just reported recently on conduct from 2025.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So it's the most effective way to change people's behavior is forward-looking, is the advice and education context and not in the annual disclosure realm.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks why adding prediction market reporting is difficult given existing disclosure requirements. Carolyn distinguishes between reporting assets held on a final date versus reporting completed in-and-out event contracts, noting nothing comparable exists.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions COIB on annual disclosure comparison

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So when we talk about the annual disclosure, because arguably there are certain things that aren't necessarily conflicts, especially when you're running as a candidate, but that pertains to anything in the annual disclosure.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Earning, owning property, the fight where you're...

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Spouse or any other works.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Like there's multiple layers of what is already disclosed.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So I'm not understanding why it would be difficult to add this one more section because there are several sections and the annual disclosure does take some time, but to the point that you made where some things are not excluded.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So why disclose them?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Why disclose the things that are currently in the disclosure?

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Well, I would argue that, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: A conflict.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Yeah, so certainly there's holding real estate.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: These are assets that you hold over the course of the reporting year or on the final day.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The current prohibition or the current reporting regime that's being articulated are...

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Event contracts that are closed, they're concluded.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So this is conduct that's being reported versus your asset.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: assets and liabilities on the final date of the reporting period.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So there's a distinction.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And there's nothing about when someone is a candidate for elective office, just for that example, one might want to know what positions they've held, where their assets are, because that's going to impact the kinds of...

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The kinds of things that might be interests of theirs as a public servant.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: In other words, if I have an affiliation with a particular business or own 10 buildings in a particular community, that might impact my concerns about how a certain community is regulated.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: But here, these are in-and-out contracts that could happen in a day or two that someone could be reporting zero or could be reporting 100 event contracts.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: There's just nothing comparable to it in the annual.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about penalty sufficiency. Ethan Carrier notes the bill would give COIB power to remove public servants from office, which it has never had. Carolyn raises concerns about enforcing against family members of public servants, which is unfamiliar territory for the board.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions COIB on penalties and family enforcement

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Suppose it does pass.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: There are multiple penalties that a person violating the law could have assessed on them.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Have you guys looked at and do you feel these penalties seem sufficient given the potential of a policymaker to use prediction markets to enrich themselves based on the events they control?

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Yeah, we have looked at the penalties that are in the proposed legislation.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: I mean, they mirror the penalties that the board has in its normal enforcement context with a couple of, I think, important differences.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: One of the differences is this would allow the board, if I'm reading it correctly, to remove a public servant from office.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: That is not a power the board has ever had before, and we don't think it's a power the board should have.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We think that that's a power that should be reserved for the entity that is, in fact, employing that public servant.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: The other distinction here is about the sort of scope of the enforcement, which is the board has never had the authority to engage in enforcement against people who are the family members.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: of public servants before.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And we're honestly not sure what all the ramifications of that might be, but certainly when thinking about conflicts of interest,

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: it's about, which is sort of the core function of the board, we're thinking about people who have duties as public servants.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: And so we're always sort of thinking about how the law applies in all respects, but maybe particularly in the enforcement respect with regard to where those public duties conflict with private interests.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So it's unfamiliar and maybe problematic territory for us to get into.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Engaging in administrative enforcement against the family members of public servants.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: That being said, we also think that the confidentiality restrictions capture, you know, the use of confidential information or disclosure of confidential information by a public servant to a family member or, in the case of disclosure to anyone,

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: in a way that would capture that family member concern.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: But the onus is on the public servant who actually has that public duty to maintain public trust, as you were describing as sort of one of the fundamental values underlying your proposed legislation and conflicts of interest law in the city.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: So that's an incredibly important value.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: We're a little concerned that maybe we've drifted beyond that when we get to

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Actually engaging in enforcement against family members of public servants.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Gotcha.

chapters.summary: A council member asks whether prediction markets could negatively affect government decision-making like real estate holdings. Carrier says it is too early to assess, and Carolyn confirms COIB has concerns about prediction markets involving public duties but believes existing law covers them.
chapters.title: Council member questions COIB on prediction markets vs real estate concerns

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Chair, can I?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Going back to the example, when we go back to the annual disclosure with, I think you used real estate as an example, like if a member holds large amounts of real estate within the area could potentially affect their governing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you think prediction markets could have this same negative effect on government decision-making or other aspects of running the city?

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Could prediction markets as a general matter?

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Certainly.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: My personal view about whether people engage in online gambling or buy event contracts, it's hard to even assess that.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: I think it's way too early to determine that.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: The goal is to ensure that once people enter public service, they are focused on serving the public.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: And I think that's distinct from conduct that occurred before they entered public service.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So overall, do the COIB have any particular concerns or do you not have concerns at this moment about the widespread use of prediction markets?

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: I mean, we definitely have a lot of concerns about the use of the involvement of public servants in prediction markets that have anything to do with public servants, public duties or confidential information.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: The subject matter of your proposed legislation is incredibly important.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: We are entirely on board.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: With the values that you're articulating here.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Arkham.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Largely what we're saying here is we think we've got you covered.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: Like we think the city's conflicts of interest law, which is based in those same values that you articulated when you were providing your introductory statement about your bill, I couldn't have said it better myself.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: I mean, but we just think that the law actually just already covers it and that ultimately what this would be is essentially redundant with the existing law.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: We think, as I said, we think that Chapter 60 of the City Charter has this covered and that the board has the ability and certainly the willingness to enforce the existing provisions.

utterances.speaker: Carolyn
utterances.text: To address the concerns that you've articulated.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's all I have, Chair.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks if this issue has come across COIB's desk. Carrier says complaints are confidential but he has not seen reporting about city public servants using insider information for prediction markets, though it could be happening. Brewer notes they are now on notice.
chapters.title: Brewer questions COIB on whether prediction market cases have arisen

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Councilmember Wilson has joined us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: One quick question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Has this issue ever come up if you're able to state?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know in some cases you can't say if you're working on any cases.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But is this something that has come across your desk at all?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, there are many people who are corrupt, I got it, and they have their own issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But is this particular one?

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We haven't, I mean, certainly as you're alluding to, complaints to the board are confidential, but certainly at least in general reporting, I haven't seen reporting about city public servants

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: utilizing their public information or their city positions to engage in prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: The committee report identifies...

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Public officials in other areas of government who've been entangled in certain arenas.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: That doesn't mean it hasn't happened here.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We just haven't seen it yet.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, but now you're on notice.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: Yes, and I think the annual disclosure piece, just to say, it's like we're very cognizant of the burdens of that reporting regime, and that's just the main concern there.

utterances.speaker: Ethan Carrier
utterances.text: We get, as you identified, a lot of concerns about why do I need to report this, and we want to just make sure that the reporting requirements are as tailored to the situation as they possibly can be.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member, for bringing this up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right, you're finished for this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: For now.

chapters.summary: Paul Seamus Ryan, Executive Director of the Campaign Finance Board, introduces the CFB's role in combating big money in politics and describes Intro 12A, which would require disclosure to the CFB when candidates or independent spenders create political materials using AI software capable of generating synthetic content.
chapters.title: CFB testimony - Ryan on CFB role and Intro 12A overview

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Campaign Finance Board, Paul Ryan, the Executive Director, and Amanda Mejia.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Hold on.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I have to sort them into...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I forget about that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They always tell the truth at CFP.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right, we will now hear from the Campaign Finance Board.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Before they testify, I will administer the oath of affirmation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right, you may begin when ready.

utterances.speaker: Amanda Molillo
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Brewer and members of the Committee on Governmental Operations.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Brewer and members of the Committee on Governmental Operations, State and Federal Legislation, and other Council members.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: My name is Paul Seamus Ryan, and I'm the Executive Director of the New York City Campaign Finance Board.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Joining me today is First Deputy Executive Director Amanda Molillo.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today regarding Intro 12A of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: The CFB is an independent, nonpartisan city agency dedicated to strengthening local democracy.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: The CFB combats the influence of big money in politics by amplifying the power of small dollar contributions from everyday New Yorkers through our matching funds program.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: We work to eliminate barriers to participation by equipping New Yorkers with the information and resources they need to get out to vote and run for office.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Through our NYC Votes initiative, the CFB engages directly with voters and provides resources to make voting more accessible.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Intro 12A, sponsored by Speaker Julie-Menin, would require disclosure to the Campaign Finance Board when candidates for local elected office or entities making local election-related

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: independent expenditures create political materials or advertisements using artificial intelligence software capable of generating synthetic content.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: The CFB previously provided testimony on an earlier version of this bill, Intro 293-2024.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: on December 4th, 2024.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: The CFB acknowledges the Council's efforts to protect the integrity of our local democratic process as artificial intelligence technologies advance.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: The CFB has a unique perspective on this topic, rooted in our dual mandate to serve candidates and voters.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: We sympathize with the perspective of both.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: We wholeheartedly share your concerns about the ways that AI technology could be used to spread false information to intentionally mislead or deceive voters about council members and other city candidates and office holders.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: At the Campaign Finance Board, we have placed a priority on learning about artificial intelligence.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Melillo and I recently completed the Harvard Kennedy School AI in Action certificate course.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: We have formed an agency-wide AI working group, and we are learning more every day.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: Nevertheless, AI technology is evolving at such a rapid pace that we cannot say with certainty how best to regulate its use in elections.

chapters.summary: Ryan appreciates that the amended bill shifts from a prohibition to a disclosure model and names CFB as the implementing agency, eliminating First Amendment vulnerabilities and clarifying enforcement responsibilities. He says CFB is well qualified as a disclosure administrator.
chapters.title: CFB testimony - Ryan on amended bill and disclosure model

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: To that end, we appreciate that the amended version of this legislation meaningfully addresses our most significant legal and implementation concerns shared at the hearing in December 2024.

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: By shifting from a prohibition to a disclosure model and naming the CFB as the agency in receipt of these disclosures, as well as naming the CFB as the agency responsible for implementing the new disclosure regime,

utterances.speaker: Paul Seamus Ryan
utterances.text: the Council eliminated the greatest vulnerabilities to First Amendment legal challenge and clarified administrative and enforcement responsibilities.

chapters.summary: Saeed reports that since September 2025, CEC held 29 workshops across 11 topics with 703 board members registered and 298 attending. Topics included parliamentary procedures, de-escalation, affordable housing, AI use, social media, open data, and disability voters' rights, with sessions posted to YouTube.

chapters.summary: Saeed describes CEC's yearly language access training for community boards, occasional translation and interpretation requests accommodated as budget allows, referrals to MOIA, and over-the-phone interpretation available to all boards through a LanguageLine contract.
chapters.title: CEC testimony - Saeed on language access services

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Responsive to the evolving needs of New York City's diverse population and to support community boards in language access,

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: we provide yearly training on best practices for implementing language services as well as how to request language services from agencies.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We do receive requests from boards from time to time for translation services, which we accommodate as budget allows.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: And for interpretation service, we refer requests to the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs or one of our trusted vendors.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Finally, the CEC continues to make over-the-phone interpretation available to all community boards through a paid contract with LanguageLine.

chapters.summary: Saeed describes offering workshops at varied times, personalized board-specific trainings, coordination with the Future of Community Boards Working Group on term limit transitions, a planned liquor license workshop, and the Better Public Meetings NYC partnership with the National Civic League conducting surveys at six community boards.
chapters.title: CEC testimony - Saeed on training accessibility and future initiatives

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We work to make training more accessible and responsive to board members'needs and interests.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We offer workshops at different times and days to meet members'busy schedules.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We also provide more personalized service for individual boards, including parliamentary procedure topics of their choosing and exclusively for their own members.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We're in regular communication with the Future of Community Boards Working Group, discussing ways to improve services to boards, including exploring approaches to the upcoming member term limits.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: At the request of Brooklyn Community Board 4 and Councilmember Gutierrez's office, we are in the process of organizing a workshop on how boards deal with liquor licenses that will consist of a panel of board members who have expertise in that area.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We expect that to occur in the fall of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Lastly, in February, we began a partnership with the National Civic League and Perfect City called Better Public Meetings NYC.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: This has included conducting surveys and interviews at six community boards, including Brooklyn 4, Queens 6, Manhattan 3 and 11, and Bronx 7 and 9.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Working with the borough president's offices, we are conducting a survey of all boards asking for their best practices and policies on public meetings.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: This coming July, we'll be hosting a public forum to discuss what we've learned so far and how we can implement the learnings.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We will also work to form peer learning groups with community board members and staff.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We look forward to reporting back on that work in the future.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your attention, and we're happy to answer any questions.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Council Member Wilson.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wilson asks if CEC has taken positions on Intro 501 or 322, and about the burden on boards facing both member term limits and potential district manager term limits. Saeed says any transition requires knowledge transfer support but CEC has not taken a position.
chapters.title: Wilson questions CEC on bills and district manager transitions

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: So we have two bills before the hearing that, before the committee that we're considering today.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Has the CEC taken a position on either of these?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We have not.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Is there, particularly with the...

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Intro 501 that would set a term limit for district managers.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: I'm curious if you think that there would be, what the burden might be on boards that are anticipating a changeover from their

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: with the term limits for existing

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: and then also pondering, and we're opining on a bill that would do that for district managers if there's concern about sort of the institutional knowledge and the ability of the boards to function with these changes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: I can just say very briefly that I think any transition requires support.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: And making sure knowledge is transferred for the institution to continue, right?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: So regardless of how that transition happens, whether it's through a term or not, I think for the institution to continue to serve the public, it's very important that a knowledge transfer is arranged for.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: So that's my opinion on that.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: I don't know if, Benjamin, you want to add anything.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks what practices CEC is exploring to help boards maintain institutional knowledge during member transitions. Saeed and Benjamin Salter describe potential citywide and borough-wide gatherings for departing members to share knowledge with incoming members, working with borough presidents.
chapters.title: Wilson questions CEC on knowledge transfer practices

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: And you mentioned you're working to assist the boards with the transitions for the term limits for the members.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Are there certain practices that you're looking to help them with to maintain that knowledge on the board?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: I think so far we've just been talking about, and I'll let Benjamin comment as well, we've just been talking about

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: opportunities for people who are well-versed to share their best practices and how we can arrange those kinds of forums as the CEC in our role and support working with the future of community boards.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: But Benjamin, do you want to add anything?

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Yeah, we have discussed potential citywide gatherings or borough-wide gatherings with board members who are leaving to share knowledge of the incoming.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We've talked to the borough presidents, letting them know we're available to help convene things.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So I think all the BPs are working towards that goal of helping the transition well.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So we're just trying to work with them and the board members themselves to make it smooth and effective.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks whether community boards reach out to CEC or borough presidents for training, noting that as Manhattan borough president she had offered all the same trainings CEC now lists except AI. Salter confirms they coordinate with borough presidents to avoid duplication and offer trainings based on board member surveys.
chapters.title: Brewer questions CEC on coordination with borough presidents

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: One question I have is the district managers and the community boards themselves, do they reach out to you?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you reach out to them?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The reason I say this, as borough president, with all due respect, I look at the list with Dr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Alden Bonilla in charge.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We had every single one of those forums in terms of trainings, except AI.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We didn't have AI at that time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Every one we had.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do the other borough presidents do that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So do the boards, are they confused about reaching out for you to train, to go to the borough presidents for training?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How do you coordinate all that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because we had all those trainings.

utterances.speaker: Amanda Molillo
utterances.text: Benjamin, do you want to answer?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He doesn't want to answer all my questions.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: No, I'll answer, of course, any questions, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know Benjamin very well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I like Benjamin, but I do not agree with this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks whether CEC has a sense of whether each borough president has a robust vetting process for community board members. Salter says all borough presidents conduct interviews and process applications, and Brewer reiterates her concern that council members lack staff for similar vetting.
chapters.title: Brewer questions CEC on borough president appointment processes

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We consult with the borough president's offices.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Your office, when you were there and it was...

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Superstar.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Superstar, for sure.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So, yes, you offered a lot of those changes, not all the BPs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I looked at that list, Benjamin, every single one of AI.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I get it.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I get it.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Not every board does that.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: You'll see that we are mandated to do a lot of land use trainings, but that is something that the borough presidents do across the board.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So we offer occasional ones, but we know that we don't want to be duplicative.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So we are asked to do certain trainings.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We look at the board members.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We ask surveys from them what they want to do, and we try to fulfill those needs.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We had heard the ad.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We had heard that it was being used and poorly, so we implemented that.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: You know, with the parliamentary procedure, which was one that we offered a lot, that is now required to be held by BPs, so we have stopped offering that one.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: You know, there's only so many trainings we can offer every year.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: All board members are busy.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They have a lot of meetings.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We basically offer sort of one a week, I think, if I did the math.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So we offer what we hear the board members asking.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then in terms of, I know you're not taking positions, I just heard, on these bills, but do you feel that, do you have any sense of,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: whether or not each borough president has a process, I know Manhattan does, for implementing, I would say, quality community board members versus just, in the old days, it was political.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It was a bad situation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So that has changed in Manhattan, I assume elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have some sense of how it works across the board, or you don't pay attention to that?

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: No, no, we talked to them about that.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I mean, certainly...

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Process.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They all do the interviews that I know of.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I don't deal as much with Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We don't talk to them as much, but happy to.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We just don't.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: But they do an interview process.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They get the applications.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
