utterances.text: We talk to them about where the applications are coming from.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So I feel that they do all have a process that is pretty robust.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because it seems to me.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: that as a council member, I know I don't have the staff to do something similar.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So that's why I feel very strongly that the process is important.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Otherwise, you will have people who are just friends and not going through vetting.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks if CEC's commission will take a position on the bills; Saeed says no, as it is beyond their scope. Brewer asks about the Future of Community Boards Working Group; Salter describes it as district managers who convene regularly, issued a report, and have called for increased funding and better land use support.
chapters.title: Brewer questions CEC on position on bills and working group

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Will your board take a position on these bills, or you're not going to as far as you know?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: You're talking about the commission.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So you see board.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: No, I don't think so.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is there some reason why not?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Why not to?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Or do you think it's up to the council to decide and not the CUC?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: I think it's, yeah, there's no specific reason.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: I think it's beyond the scope of what we are.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Asked to do so, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the future of community boards working group, what does it achieve?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just don't know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's my ignorance.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They convene on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Obviously, they issued that report a while ago.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They helped do the term limit session two years ago that you were a part of and that we helped fund.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They meet regularly to make recommendations for things.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I think they've called for increased funding.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: They've called for better land use, but I'm not aware of the tangible.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: achievements.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: But I find them a useful, obviously, group to talk to.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And who's on that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that community board members and so on?

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: It's DMs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Multi-district managers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about language access request volume and whether CEC advocates for community board funding. Saeed says they receive occasional requests, and Salter notes boards' biggest frustration is lack of funding for language outreach. Saeed confirms funding advocacy is a different process outside CEC's role.
chapters.title: Brewer questions CEC on language access and funding advocacy

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the other question I have is, do you get a lot of requests for language?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have to refer to MOIA quite a bit?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How does that work?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because I've

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, that would be good if you did.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just didn't know.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Yeah, I don't think we are getting a lot.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: We do get them occasionally.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: Benjamin, do you want to add?

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I mean, we get one or two a month of somebody asking for interpretation or translation services.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah, we should be doing more because then that would bring more people into the community.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I think that's one of the biggest frustrations that boards have is the lack of ability and funding to do that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: To do the outreach for that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And do you advocate for community boards to get more funding, or that would be something that would come out of a different process?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That would not be what CEC would do?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: It's a different process.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Different process.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about training participation across boards and whether trainings are all virtual. Saeed says trainings are mostly virtual with some in-person, and attendance varies by topic and board. Brewer raises concerns about knowledge loss from member term limits, particularly on land use, and asks what plans exist for knowledge transfer. Salter says they are still developing the process.
chapters.title: Brewer questions CEC on training participation and term limit knowledge loss

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My other question is,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are you finding that there's a good, you gave some good numbers in terms of those who are participating in your trainings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you find that, like, do you find that all the community boards are getting trained?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it some that do more than others?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How do you feel about the trainings?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Like, is it across the board that people participate?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are they all Zoom?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are they in person?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Just give me a better sense of the trainings.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: The trainings are all virtual, and I think that the attendance really varies by topic and by board as well.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Saeed
utterances.text: I mean, I think we are seeing that over time there are board members who are coming to multiple trainings, so interested in different topics, and I'll let Benjamin add to this as well.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We do provide some in-person, like tonight I'm going to the bar.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: President of the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We're doing in-person de-escalation training.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: And we used to do the parliamentary procedure in person before that was handed off.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I think we have a dedicated...

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Followers.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I don't think every DM does share the information.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: We do share it with whatever board members directly that allow us to use their emails when they sign up.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: But I don't think every board, and we do keep track of every board that attends.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So we would like more, obviously.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then this issue of term limits.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So obviously a board member can come back after a year if they so choose or if they get reappointed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's the other issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know that that's going to happen much, to be honest with you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But my question is this notion that you can share data or share not just data but information, particularly on land use.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think it's a bit false because it just doesn't work like that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You've got to be in the room when the developer's there to negotiate, as I have done 215 times on terms of ULERPs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Nobody else has done that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so you have to have somebody there to say, you know, this AMI is no good or whatever.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So what plans do you really have to try to share the data or share the expertise that is going to be lost with this stupid charter revision that I hate?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Term limits for community boards I hate.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: I think that, again, sort of working off the peer learning, I mean, that's one direct.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: in-person conversations, but we really want to support what the borough presidents and the boards themselves want to do.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: So we'll be working on that over the next several months.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so you don't know yet what the process will be for doing that?

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Not the exact process, no.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't have any other questions, so thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Benjamin Salter
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer transitions to public testimony, calling Alex Camarda from Reinvent Albany and Ben Weinberg from Citizens Union, giving them three minutes each but allowing flexibility.
chapters.title: Brewer calls public testimony panel - Reinvent Albany and Citizens Union

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We're going to take testimony from the public now.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Our first testimony is from...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Alex Camara, Reinvent Albany, and also Ben Weinberg from Citizens Union, and you have three minutes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But you can talk longer because I know you both can.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Come on up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm not supposed to say that for the record, but I am.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Whoever would like to start, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Ben Weinberg of Citizens Union commends the intent of Intro 951 but argues much of the conduct is already covered by Chapter 68 of the City Charter. He recommends addressing gaps through existing law, removing penalties on relatives, and suggests alternatives like prohibiting participation in prediction markets related to official duties.
chapters.title: Weinberg Citizens Union on Intro 951 - prediction markets

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Oh, wait, where's my, here we go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Good morning, council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you, chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: My name is Ben Weinberg.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: I'm the director of public policy at Citizens Union, a nonpartisan good government group that works to advance honest and accountable government and fair and open elections in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: I will largely comment on the, on intro 951 on prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: The campaign finance board have provided a pretty thoughtful testimony on Intro 12A, the one on AI-generated campaign content.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: So on Intro 951, we commend Councilmember Thomas Henry for recognizing the ethical risks posed by prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: When public officials can profit from outcomes they influence or know about before the public does, there is a clear risk of conflicts of interest and abuse of insider information, as the prominent examples mentioned before in the committee's report indicate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: However, we believe much of the content, much of the conduct, sorry, the bill seeks to prohibit is already covered by the city's code.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: conflicts of interest law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Chapter 68 prohibits public servants from using their positions for financial gain and from disclosing confidential information to advance their private interest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Those provisions apply to all public servants, not just policymaker as the bill does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: For that reason, we recommend addressing any remaining gaps through Chapter 68 rather than creating a separate regulatory framework.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Building on the city's existing ethics laws and its definitions would make enforcement clear and more effective.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: We also recommend removing provisions that would impose penalties on relatives of public servants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: The obligation to protect the integrity of public office should rest with the public servants, and extending the COIB's enforcement authority to spouses or parents or siblings would raise practical and legal concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Now, if the council does determine that existing law is insufficient in addressing the new risks posed by prediction markets, it could consider other alternatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Our written testimony offers some options.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: For example, one could prohibit public servants from participating in prediction markets when the event, so-called event, is related to their official duties, whether they...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Whether they participated in it or not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: The North Carolina governor issued a similar executive order recently.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: One could tailor a specific definition of non-public information

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: that's relevant to this context if the current definition of confidential information doesn't cover all cases related to prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: You could also require written affirmation of existing restrictions so that every public servant will have to kind of sign that they acknowledge that restriction and that provides for

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: a way to enforce that form, basically signing that form later on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: That's all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: I'm actually done with that one, so I'm happy to take any other questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Alex Camarda of Reinvent Albany supports disclosure of AI-altered political communications but urges the bill require disclosure in the communication itself, not just to CFB. He raises concerns about intersection with state election law, undefined terms, and whether provenance data will be available to voters in real time.
chapters.title: Camarda Reinvent Albany on Intro 12A - AI disclosure

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Brewer and members of the Council Committee on Governmental Operations, State and Federal Legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: My name is Alex Camarda.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: I'm a senior policy advisor for Reinvent Albany.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Reinvent Albany is a watchdog organization that advocates for open and accountable government in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Reinvent Albany supports the intent and spirit of both Intro 12A and Intro 951, but believes the Council needs to better account for how these proposals intersect with the existing law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Regarding Intro number 12A, Reinvent Albany supports disclosure of when political communications are created by artificial intelligence or altered by it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: The advent of social media and more recently artificial intelligence have made it very challenging for New Yorkers to discern between the truth and fiction in digital media related to candidates running for office in political and public policy discourse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Social media platforms are awash in communications where

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: The sources are not clearly identifiable, and facts and untruths mingle together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: The growing use of artificial intelligence compounds this already significant problem with videos, sound, and pictures reflecting reality altered to create a false impression with the viewer often unaware of the manipulation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Intro number 12A takes a first step in addressing the growing challenges to voters making informed choices about candidates by requiring provenance data, underlying metadata indicating when political communications were altered and by what tool

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: to be disclosed to the New York City Campaign Finance Board and made available to the public online.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: While not explicitly indicated in the bill language, this disclosure appears to be implemented in concert with CFB's disclosure regime and its rules, and subject to the existing enforcement procedures and penalties,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: the latter of which is referenced in the bill summary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: While Reinvent Albany commends the Speaker for tackling this issue, it is also imperative that the bill at minimum clarify that disclosure is required in the communication itself that simply states

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: this image, video, or audio has been manipulated consistent with New York State election law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Reinvent Albany will only support legislation that contains this disclosure in the communication itself because it ensures that voters are informed in real time and can adequately process the message to make an informed consideration of the candidate running for office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Beyond the recommended...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: disclosure in the communication.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: There are several other issues the council should clarify or consider before passing this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Some embodied in Governor Hochul's fiscal year 27 budget proposals and contemplated in briefs on the issue by national experts like the Brennan Center for Justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Specifically, the council should clarify in the bill summary or legislative intent how this legislation builds on or intersects with New York State election law 14-106,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: which already requires disclosure of, quote, material deceptive media, with few exceptions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Adequately define synthetic content creation system and provenance data as the bill references section 1550 of the New York State General Business Law, which currently doesn't exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: In determining these definitions, the council should consider whether to include personal identifying information, for example, as part of the provenance data, such as an internet protocol address.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Charge synthetic content creation system providers like OpenAI and Meta with providing a tool to the public to read provenance data and political and policy-related communications, as is mandated in the New York State Fiscal 27 Executive Budget Proposals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Prohibit the creation or publication of a deepfake within 90 days of an election if the publisher knows or should know it was fake.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: It was intended to impact the election and was made without a candidate's or individual's permission if they are featured in the deepfake, as was done in the executive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: budget proposals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: This will require examining constitutional permissibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: And clarify if copies of communications will be provided to the campaign finance board and made public with prominence data for communications sent by candidate committees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: It's our understanding that unlike independent expenditure committees, candidate committees are not required to submit originals or copies of their campaign communications until the post-election audit process and are not posted publicly in the searchable database.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: If Providence data is not provided until well after voters have cast their ballots or is provided without a copy of the communication, a voter can reference.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: When reviewing the data, the utility of this disclosure requirement will be greatly diminished.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: And lastly, amend Section 1 of the bill to not only reference new additions to the campaign finance database this bill requires, but also other disclosures beyond contributions and expenditures of a campaign's authorized committee.

chapters.summary: Camarda urges the council to rework Intro 951 to align with Chapter 68 of the City Charter rather than creating a diverging enforcement regime with different definitions of policymaker and relative. He notes the bill's $50 reporting threshold is far below the current $1,000 income reporting threshold.
chapters.title: Camarda on Intro 951 - alignment with existing law

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Regarding Intro 951, we support the intent of this bill to clarify that public officials may not use confidential insider knowledge gained from public employment to profit in prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Governor Hochul recently enacted Executive Order 60 that prohibits state employees from profiting from inside information that they learn while working as public servants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: We support that ban because it clarifies the state code of ethics, public officers law section 74, and provides clear guidance to public servants serving under the governor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Intro 951 in its current form would create a diverging regime outside of Chapter 68 of the City Charter, the City Ethics Code, which, as referenced, already prohibits

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: using confidential information for personal gain under Section 2604.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: We urge the City Council to rework this legislation to ensure that it does not create a different enforcement regime from other prohibited conduct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Chapter 68 already prohibits profiting from confidential information in Section 2604B4.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: If the City Council wishes to explicitly include prediction marks in this prohibition, it should amend this section rather than include a new section of law in the Administrative Code with different definitions and covered individuals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Specifically, 2604B4 provides that public servants cannot disclose confidential information that is, quote, obtained as a result of the official duties of such public servant and which are

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: which is not otherwise available to the public, and they cannot, quote, use any such information to advance any direct or indirect financial or other private interest of the public servant or of any other person or firm associated with the public servant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Intro 951 would create a new Chapter 12 in Title III of the Administrative Code that diverges from some of the standards in Chapter 68 of the City Charter and Section 12-110 of the Administrative Code, the Annual Disclosure Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Specifically, it creates new definitions of policymaker and relative.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Chapter 68 applies to all public servants and defines those associated with public servants, including children, spouses, domestic partners, and persons with whom the public servant has business or financial relationships.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Intro 951 only includes policymakers and their relatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Relatives under this bill include all those listed in Chapter 68 under associated, but add step-parents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: It would impose fines upon the relatives of policymakers who profit from inside information and prediction markets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Chapter 68, in contrast, only provides penalties for public servants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: And the bill adds new reporting requirements for financial disclosure statements requiring a listing of all transactions of $50 or more for prediction market transactions, event contracts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Currently, the thresholds for reporting income start at $1,000.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: We also note that the bill excludes event contracts issued on platforms regulated by the New York State Gaming Commission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: While this is likely an attempt to differentiate,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: between sports betting authorized by New York State and betting related to city government or political actions that are regulated by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: We caution that New York should decide at some point to regulate these markets.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks both witnesses about their positions on including relatives in enforcement. Weinberg says existing law already covers relatives through the public servant's duty, and Camarda focuses on alignment with existing law rather than specific positions on relatives.
chapters.title: Brewer Q&A with Weinberg and Camarda on relatives and enforcement

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Thank you, and I welcome any questions you may have.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you both.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think you're sort of saying, both of you, something similar to what the agency stated, basically, that Amy Klobuchar's bill in Washington sounds like it's what we're aiming for.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was one thing that I came out.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then secondly, you like the concept, obviously we all do, that public officials stay to the law and not try to break it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I think...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Ben, you were stating that you don't think relatives should be involved, and I wasn't clear what Reinvent Albany was saying about that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: On 951, I think our overarching recommendation is that the bill needs to align with existing law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: It's creating...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Which does not include relatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I'm commenting generally, but if the council wants to add prediction markets to the existing law, we think it should amend the sections that relate to existing law rather than creating a separate enforcement regime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: You know, as far as the relatives question goes, I don't think we weigh in specifically as to whether who should be included as relatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: But I think.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: I think we're more focused on just alignment.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll just say relatives are included in existing provisions to the extent that they profit from something that the public servant does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: And then the enforcement and the investigation is directed towards the public servant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: The assumption is that they had or may have something to do with the gain that their relative had.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: So the enforcement in that case would remain the same.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks whether the bill should focus on images, video, and audio rather than fundraising emails. Both witnesses agree, with Camarda emphasizing the need to clarify how the bill intersects with existing state election law on material deceptive media, and Weinberg noting enforcement challenges under state law.
chapters.title: Brewer Q&A on AI scope and state law intersection

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I think you're both stating in terms of the issue of...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: AI, that the image, video, and audio is where we should stick and not go into the campaign fundraising letters and so on and so forth, right?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I would agree with that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Yeah, I'd say for 12A, our principal question really to the council is how does this intersect with existing New York state law?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: You know, there are the provisions that I referenced in the testimony that relate to a disclosure of material deceptive communications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Those are pretty narrow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: I think they align with a lot of the recommendations that the CFB made in their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: So if the disclosure of the communications is meant to align with...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: State law, I think that will address a lot of the concerns the CFB had, but that's not clear to us from the bill, if that's the intent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: But that's what we think it should work on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: There's a larger question of enforcement related to that law, state law, that requires candidates and campaigns to basically stamp.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: This was generated by AI because enforcement of election law is a difficult matter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: It's conducted by the State Board of Elections and by the Division of Enforcement within the State Board of Elections.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: And both of these bodies have been prone to delays and other issues with enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: The state law actually allows candidates to petition the court to do something about it if their likelihood is part of that campaign or that ad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: But that law still applies to New York City candidates and campaigns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: So in that sense, there wouldn't be much of a difference.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah, the CFB said, I thought it was an honest answer, that to do it, they'd have to have a vendor and they'd have to have expertise and they'd have to hire people who are paid more, who are expertise in this area.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I thought they were honest about what's involved, if they're really going to do the...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Investigation and keep people accountable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not as easy as one might think.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: You know, I sometimes think about this as similar to the post-employment restrictions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: It's difficult, almost impossible for the regulating agency to find all cases where someone violated a post-employment restriction because they're not monitoring the actions of thousands and thousands of...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: So...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: It's based on reporting of violations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: And in that case, that would probably look the same.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you both very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ben Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: John Weinberg, general counsel to Manhattan Borough President Brad Hoylman-Siegel, testifies in opposition to Intro 501, joined by Bronx and Queens borough presidents. He argues district managers are professional public servants whose institutional knowledge is indispensable, and mandatory turnover would weaken community boards.
chapters.title: John Weinberg Manhattan BP opposes Intro 501

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Jonathan Weinberg, Manhattan Borough President's Office.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You have to be sworn in because you're representing the borough president.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right, we will now hear testimony from the Manhattan Borough President's Office.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Before we begin, I will administer the oath of affirmation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee, and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right, you may begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Brewer, and thank you to the other members of the council and the members of the public gathered here today.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: I'm here on behalf of Manhattan Borough President Brad Hoylman-Siegel.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: My name is John Weinberg.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: I'm general counsel to the borough president, and I'm here to enter into the record official testimony in opposition to Intro 501 on behalf of Borough President Hoylman-Siegel.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: And this written testimony is also assigned on to by Bronx Borough President Vanessa Gibson and Queens Borough President Donovan Richards.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: President Hoylman-Siegel has significant concerns regarding Intro 501, which is drafted would undermine the institutional effectiveness of New York City's community.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: Intro 501 would impose term limits on community board district managers.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: District managers are professional public servants whose institutional knowledge and long-standing relationships with residents, agencies, and stakeholders are indispensable to the effective operation of community boards.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: These positions are not political appointments, nor should they be treated as such.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: At a time when land use and service delivery issues are becoming increasingly complex, community boards benefit from experienced administrative leadership.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: Mandatory turnover would unnecessarily weaken continuity, diminish expertise,

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: and impair the ability for boards to effectively serve their communities.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: Community board members have universally told us that district managers are quite literally the glue that hold community boards together.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: We have heard no concerns whatsoever from our constituents on the performance and dedication of these district managers.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: So why disturb something that is working so well?

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: We know Chair Brewer and other members of the council understand firsthand the important role that community boards play and that district managers specifically play in New York City civil life.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: We therefore respectively urge the council to reconsider the bill in its current form.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: Thank you for your time and attention to this matter.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate this, your testimony, and we will certainly take it under consideration.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Brewer.

utterances.speaker: John Weinberg
utterances.text: We appreciate your time and attention.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer calls the next panel: Susan Stetzer from Manhattan CB3, Mark Diller from Manhattan CB2, and Rafael Moray-Pernet from Bronx CB6.
chapters.title: Brewer calls district manager panel

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The next speaker, Susan Stetzer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Muni Board 3, Mark Dillard, Muni Board 2, Raphael Moore-Pundit, who is Board 6 in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Whoever would like to begin, go ahead, and thank you for being here.

chapters.summary: Susan Stetzer, district manager for Manhattan CB3 for 22 years, opposes Intro 501, arguing there is no precedent for an elected official to terminate staff at a different city agency. She says four-year terms would drive district managers to find stable jobs and that existing HR procedures for hiring and firing are sufficient.
chapters.title: Stetzer CB3 Manhattan opposes Intro 501

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I'll go first.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: My name is Susan Stetzer.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I'm district manager for Manhattan Community Board 3.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I'd just like to mention that we're actually city agencies, not the public.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I want to thank you for this opportunity to share concerns regarding proposed legislation and budget.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: Because of the limited time, I will speak to Intro 501, which is the most shocking.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: The Community Board 3 Executive Committee voted last Thursday to oppose legislation into 322 and 501.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: This will be voted on tomorrow night and submitted to the committee as testimony.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: And I'd like to say I actually agree with Councilmember Williams that our terms of employment should be clarified.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: And I'd like to suggest that the council can go on our website under...

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: Resources and tests and standards and see very detailed lengthy responsibilities that the board voted on for the district manager.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: It is on a managerial test and standards form provided by DCAS.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I'd also like to say that I will not personally be impacted by this, as I've been in the position for 22 years and expect to be retired well.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: within the next four years.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: City Charter provides each community board within budgetary appropriations shall appoint a district manager, as it deems appropriate, who shall serve at the pleasure of the community board.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: There seems to be diverse opinions on whether this would require a charter revision commission to allow borough presidents to terminate a district manager, as this would diminish the power of a city agency.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: There is no precedent in New York City government for an elected official to terminate a staff person at a different city agency outside of their direct line of authority.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: The immediate and long-term impact of a four-year hire would be that district managers would leave community boards to find stable jobs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: The board's abilities to hire for this position would be materially impaired.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: District manager value is in long-term institutional knowledge of the community and the board, especially important as boards transition to member term limits.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: Community boards already have the usual human resource procedures to hire and fire district managers.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: We're subject to, as I mentioned, to New York City managerial tasks and standards.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: Boards can evaluate and, when necessary, terminate district managers.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: And I would say I've been evaluated against the tasks and standards by several.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: Chairs are from my board, and several chairs have done this several times.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: So the process is absolutely in place.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: It's the same process used by agencies for all mid-level managers and the same process used by elected officials for their staff.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I won't go into what we do because you've already mentioned that.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: For borough presidents to have the power to remove district managers who are carrying out the wishes of their boards and the community at large

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: would put district managers in a bind between carrying out the directives of their board and those of borough presidents when the directives and positions differ.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: I'm not aware of any reason to treat district managers different than any other city manager, and I'm not aware of any borough president seeking the ability to remove district managers.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: And I didn't have time to talk about the budget, but I would just like to add that community boards have not had an increase to their

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: other than personal services in many years.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: And with the technology and training needed these days, we are in dire need of budget increases.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Rafael Moray-Pernet, district manager for Bronx CB6, requests a baseline budget increase for community boards, citing growing responsibilities including zoning amendments, open dining permits, cannabis licenses, and traffic redesigns. He calls Intro 501A an attempt to politicize the district manager position.
chapters.title: Moray-Pernet CB6 Bronx on budget increase and 501

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: Good morning, Council Member and Council.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: My name is Rafael Moray-Pernet.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: I'm the District Manager for Community Board 6 in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: I'm here representing the Community Board who adopted this position at a board meeting.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: We would like to ask the Council for a baseline budget increase to Community Boards.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: The responsibilities of community boards have grown over time.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: We recently opined on several citywide zoning tax amendments, which created a new universal affordability program, which is

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: creating more as of right development while still including community board review of that development.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: We are now pining on open dining permits, retail cannabis licenses.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: We are pining on open street applications, city charter amendments, as well as our ongoing regular duties of responding to constituent complaints of agency service delivery,

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: ULERP actions, liquor licenses, and traffic redesign projects.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: So if the various recent legislative changes to the scope of the community boards have increased the amount of work that the boards are responsible for doing, it would make sense that the council would allocate more funding to community boards to support this work.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: Most of our budget is used for a salary to support someone answering the phone when a constituent calls the office about a pothole or a tree, et cetera, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: We ask that the council consider this and push forward in the current budget process.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: The council has shown time and time again that you value community input and that what the city is attempting to streamline and centralize land use procedures and other processes, that community input is valuable.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: And in order for community input to be valuable, community boards need to be properly staffed to field those complaints and hear from the community on local issues.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: And then lastly, my personal capacity, I want to just reiterate that Intro 501A is an attempt to politicize the district manager position and is very much antithetical to the value of community input at a local level.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Mark Diller, district manager for Manhattan CB2 and former board chair, opposes Intro 501, arguing that as-of-right development makes community board work more nuanced and requires legacy knowledge. He says it took years to build community trust and that four-year terms would create instability when member term limits take effect.
chapters.title: Diller CB2 Manhattan opposes Intro 501

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: My name is Mark Diller.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I am the district manager of Community Board 2 in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I'm also a former chair of a community board, so I bring two perspectives to the issues before this committee.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I have submitted on behalf of Community Board 2 our resolution from last year regarding what was then Intro 1065, which has the salient provisions are virtually identical to 501.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: My colleagues have already identified many of the issues that I wanted to address, so I'll try to streamline.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: There are two key aspects to a district manager's position, obviously some looking inward and some looking outward.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Those looking inward are to support the board members in their work on things like ULERPs, which are increasingly rare because of actions like the charter amendments and the city of...

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Yes, requiring ever more nuanced abilities to bring in legacy information.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: The removal of the creation of as of right development opportunities makes the community boards work in trying to find benefits for the public more nuanced, more complex and requires history and legacy information to be brought to the bear.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: So that is so and then looking outward being a resource for constituent services I'm three and a half years into being a district manager Which I thought I would know how to do having been a chair of a board, but boy is it different and

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And it takes time to build those relationships in the community, to be trusted by community organizations.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: My board has a very active constituency, and active sometimes means doubting, sometimes means you need to earn their respect and trust.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I'm on my way.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Maybe two more years I'll be off probation, but that's something that is antithetical to the idea of a four-year term.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: So Intro 501 would create instability in a place where we need it more and more, especially as board member term limits take effect.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Switching to the option or the issue of borough president termination, I have the greatest respect for the current Manhattan borough president to whom we look for guidance.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: But this is a long-term.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: issue and as my colleague has said politicizing the office of district manager is not in the service of the community's needs.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Finally, again to talk about the borough president's role,

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: The current borough president has followed in Councilmember Brewer's footsteps in terms of enhancing the outreach and meaningful engagement of recruiting and discerning who should be a board member.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And trying to replicate that across 51 council districts is neither feasible nor is it a good use of public money.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: A better use would be the increase in the community board budget that my colleague just advocated.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks for specific budget increase numbers; Moray-Pernet suggests $50,000 per board, Stetzer says at least $30,000, and Diller says $50,000 is the low end. Brewer also asks about positions on Intro 322; Stetzer's executive committee voted unanimously to oppose it, while the others have not taken it up.
chapters.title: Brewer Q&A with DM panel on budget amounts and 322 positions

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I guess one question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm glad you're all against these, at least 501.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You didn't talk much about the other bill, and I'll ask about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But what would be the amount?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because I have been advocating for more money for community boards, but I haven't heard a lot of specifics.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is there an amount that you are suggesting, or is there...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's often staffing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I laugh a little bit because Manhattan has had ULERPs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We've had street issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We've had liquor issues for years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And now, ha-ha, every other borough is stuck with it, too.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question, is there some numbers that I'm asking?

utterances.speaker: Rafael Moray-Pernet
utterances.text: My board has requested a $50,000 budget increase per community board.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so that would be, that's what.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That would be.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Be something that others, will others have numbers or do you think that's appropriate?

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: My board has not voted on a number, but I would say it would have to be at least 30.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: And the reason for that is we can keep a lot of our staff.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: There's not even equity within city government.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: So some of the money needs to go to pay the staff members as well as OTPS for the boards.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I would think 50 is a low end of the spectrum.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And the reason is that now you're talking to the two district managers who have the most liquor licenses in captivity.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: So we need a whole nother staff person.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: We have my state liquor authority committee.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I have two of them.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Each of them meet once a month, including all 12 months.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And we typically get between 60 and 70 30-day notices every month.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: I need another staff person.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And by the way, I need the ability to use, right now I have consultants who I have to pay OTPS, but it requires council member approval in order to move more than enough money to pay that person.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And so I need a whole full-time staff member.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: So $50,000 is kind of on the low end of what we would want to get.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: And as my colleague has mentioned, we do a great job of training city employees who are then snapped up for higher-paying jobs elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I will advocate for that, but we probably need some more help in doing so.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm just telling you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Now, in terms of 322, have your board...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's voted on that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know you have on D501, 322 being the appointment process.

utterances.speaker: Susan Stetzer
utterances.text: My executive committee voted on a Thursday night unanimously to oppose it, and it will go to full board tomorrow night.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Anybody else?

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: CB2 has not taken up that issue as of yet.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mark Diller
utterances.text: Board 6 has not either in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Well, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And we will work ongoing with some of these issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer calls the next panel: Robert Camacho and Celestina Leong from Brooklyn CB4, Laura Singer from Brooklyn CB15, and Irsa Weatherspoon from Brooklyn CB8.
chapters.title: Brewer calls Brooklyn community board panel

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Next is Camillo, looks like robot from Bushwick.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Celestina Leon, Community Board 4 in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Laura Singer, Board 15 in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And Ursa Weatherspoon, Brooklyn 8.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And whomever would like to start, whenever you're ready.

chapters.summary: Laura Singer, district manager for Brooklyn CB15, opposes Intro 501, arguing no city agency manager has term limits and that term limits would deter talented candidates. She says giving borough presidents firing power over staff they do not hire is illogical and that the bill targets community boards for further weakening.
chapters.title: Singer CB15 Brooklyn opposes 501

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Good afternoon, my name is Laura Singer and I'm District Manager for Brooklyn Community Ward 15, which is Sheepshead Bay, Gerritsen Beach, Manhattan Beach, Homecrest, and Madison.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: I'm speaking in regards to Intro 501.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: What city agency manager has a term limit?

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: What agency manager would take a job outside of perhaps an elected official if they could potentially lose it in four years'time?

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Not all of us have that calling to be an elected official, but we still want to serve our community, and so this is the path that we chose.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: By placing term limits on district managers, we wind up in actuality denying ourselves of some truly talented people who would never consider a position with a potential term limit.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Currently, resourcing and attracting staff with our very small budgets has been challenging.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Now with the caveat of not being extended past the four-year term, we'd only further turn potential hires off.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Board members with historic knowledge are now being termed out, so you want to take a potential longstanding staff member with institutional knowledge and place them on the chopping block as well.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: To what end?

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: With the potential for a constant turnover of district managers, you run the risk of having new hires using crucial time learning the job and focusing on procedures, giving them less time to devote to actually serving their community.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: There's a natural progression and turnover that occurs.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Limiting a person's capacity to serve will prove more of a detriment to those who truly care and want to make a positive impact.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Boards have bylaws that determine how employees are hired and terminated.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Giving an outside agency such as the borough president's office who does not resource or hire the ability to fire a district manager seems quite frankly illogical.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Unfortunately, as of late, it seems to have been open season on community boards, with legislation being created each and every day to silence that voice.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: This bill is no different.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: It's just targeting another component of the community board, a key staff member that oversees its management, which is just looking to further rip community boards asunder.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: As elected officials, you are entrusted to serve your communities to the best of your abilities.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: You should be visionaries to bring about positive change and amplify your community's voice, not silence it.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: This legislation just serves to encasitate community boards further, and I urge you as champions for your communities to let Bill Intro 501 go no further than this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Laura Singer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Who wants to go next?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Camarda
utterances.text: Yes, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Celestina Leong, district manager for Brooklyn CB4, says boards need consistent support during term limit transitions and questions how institutional knowledge will be retained. On Intro 322, she notes the current structure leaves room for politicization and encourages considering alternatives like civic lottery processes.
chapters.title: Leong CB4 Brooklyn on 501 and 322

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Brewer and Council.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I'm Celestina Leong.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I'm the District Manager for Brooklyn Community Board 4, representing Bushwick.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: And coming to the Community Board for a professional opportunity was unexpected for me, but it was a way to come back and serve my community after my family was displaced from the area.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: The Community Board that hired me in 2017 is not the same board that I work for now.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: We have to deal with the added layer of the transition from gentrification, which is something that's repeated itself over time in the city, but is still new to Bushwick.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I am also a founder of the Future of Community Boards Working Group.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I was very eager to learn as a new district manager, and who else better to learn from than my colleagues who have been doing this work for a very long time.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I owe a lot of what I know now to them and to also the board members who prepared me for this role.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I was facilitating that group from 2019 to about 2026 this year, when due to capacity limitations being the one full-time staff member prevented me from doing so.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: But I am encouraged and empowered by my board to continue to do this work.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: In regards to Intro 501, while I can appreciate the need for structure, I believe the boards need consistent support and preparation to manage staff, especially as we experience term limits.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: There are inconsistent practices and access to information across the boroughs that change with each term, with each borough president's administration.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I ask, how are we retaining information or requiring transitional documentation to support incoming members?

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: and elected officials.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: This is something that's been discussed preliminarily with colleagues in the Civic Engagement Commission while preparing on a citywide level for term limits.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: In reference to Intro 322 for councilmember appointments, to my knowledge, the borough president will have the ability to appoint when the council seat is vacant, and this is something that will address a situation that occurred in the past during the pandemic at CB4.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: One councilmember had resigned, and half of the board wasn't reappointed when a special election was canceled until that vacant seat was filled, which impacted representation on the board.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: Alternatively, while the current appointment structure is longstanding, it leaves room for potential politicization.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I encourage city leaders to consider alternatives or other amendments that further empower community members while holding them accountable.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: And there's an example you will hear.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: Our precinct community and clergy councils have a process and also considering a civic lottery process in the future.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: I encourage and remain hopeful for greater collaboration with the board slash board citywide.

utterances.speaker: Celestina Leong
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Who's next?

chapters.summary: Irsa Weatherspoon, chair of Brooklyn CB8, testifies that her board voted unanimously to oppose Intro 501, arguing it undermines community board authority and independence. She says existing bylaws already provide accountability procedures and that borough president removal authority is inconsistent with community board structure.
chapters.title: Weatherspoon CB8 Brooklyn opposes 501

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chairwoman Brewer and members of the Governmental Operations State and Federal Legislation Committee and those in attendance.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: My name is Irsa Weatherspoon, Chairperson of Brooklyn Community Board 8, and I thank you for the opportunity to provide my testimony at today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: On June 11, 2026, the members of Brooklyn Community Board 8 voted unanimously to oppose Intro 501, the proposed local law that

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: would amend the New York City Charter by imposing four-year term limits on community board district managers, subject to reappointment, and by granting Bower presidents the authority to remove district managers at will.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Brooklyn Community Board 8 believes that both provisions undermine the authority, independence, and effectiveness of community boards.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Under the New York City Charter, district managers are city employees who are hired by and serve at the pleasure of their respective community boards.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: They are accountable to the boards that recruit.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Evaluate and supervise them, not to the city council or board presidents.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: By imposing arbitrary term limits on district managers, Intro 501 diminishes the discretion of community board members and interferes with the ability to retain experienced and effective administrative leadership.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Moreover, Community Board 8's bylaws already establish clear procedures for evaluating performance, addressing concerns, and when necessary, removing a district manager for cause.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: These safeguards provide accountability while preserving the board's authority to manage its own personnel decisions.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: The proposal to grant Boa President's unilateral authority to remove district managers is particularly concerning, allowing an elected official to dismiss an employee whom they've never hired nor directly supervised

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: is inconsistent with the structure, purpose, and independence of community boards.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Such authority would disregard the extensive time, effort, and deliberation that boards invest in recruiting, selecting, and overseeing qualified district managers.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: At a time when community board members themselves are subject to turn limits, the importance of experienced district managers cannot be overstated.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Long-serving district managers provide critical institutional knowledge, continuity, and stability.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: They cultivate longstanding relationships with city agencies, city elected officials, community-based organizations, houses of worship, local businesses, and residents.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: These relationships strengthen the board's ability to advocate effectively for the communities it serves and ensure

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: the important initiatives and concerns addressed without interruption.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: District managers are professional administrators whose performance should be evaluated by the community boards that employ them.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Intro 501 appears to treat these positions more like elected offices, subject to periodic

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: political review rather than professional management roles grounded in expertise, experience, and accountability of the board.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: For these reasons, Brooklyn Community Board 8 respectfully urges the Committee on Governmental Operations, State and Federal Legislation to oppose Intro 501

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: and preserve the authority of community boards to select, supervise, and when necessary, remove their own district managers.

utterances.speaker: Irsa Weatherspoon
utterances.text: Thank you kindly for your time and consideration.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.
