chapters.summary: Robert Camacho, chair of Brooklyn CB4 for 42 years, passionately opposes term limits for both board members and district managers, saying he is being forced out despite dedicating decades to his community. He argues the bills would politicize boards and that existing processes already allow removal of non-performing members.
chapters.title: Camacho CB4 chair opposes term limits and politicization

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about positions on Intro 322 and the Brooklyn appointment process. Panelists describe a process where applications and interviews occur but new members sometimes have never attended a board meeting. Weatherspoon notes the best members come from the community attending meetings.
chapters.title: Brewer Q&A with Brooklyn panel on 322 and appointment process

chapters.summary: Sandy McKee, chair of Manhattan CB6, calls Intro 501 a solution in search of a problem, arguing no one has shown a need for it. She says institutional knowledge matters more than ever with member term limits approaching and that the bill was developed without consulting community boards.
chapters.title: McKee CB6 Manhattan opposes 501

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Brewer and members of the Committee on Governmental Operations, State and Federal Legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: My name is Sandy McKee and I am the Chair of Manhattan Community Board 6.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: I'm here to state CB6's opposition to Intro 501.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Manhattan Community Board 6 opposed the earlier version of this measure at this committee's oversight hearing last June, and our position has not changed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Intro 501 is a solution in search of a problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: No one has shown a need for it, and what it would actually do is introduce instability into community boards and inject politics into a role that is meant to be free of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: The strength of a community board comes from two things, its independence and the deep institutional knowledge built up in its district office and members over many years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: That knowledge of the district's history, its agencies, and its people takes years to build, and it is often the difference between a community board that gets a community problem that gets solved and one that doesn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: That institutional knowledge matters now more than ever.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: With term limits for community board members taking effect in less than a year, members will soon begin turning over far more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: And in the continuity that holds a board together,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: together will rest less and less on the members alone and increasingly on the district manager's shoulders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Disrupting that continuity on an arbitrary schedule doesn't reform anything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: It discards the very thing that makes the board effective, and it is the residents we serve who will pay the price.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: If there were real concerns somewhere about a district manager, we would want it addressed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: But the remedy for an isolated concern is not an amendment to the city charter that affects all 59 community boards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Intro 501 reaches into every district in the city to solve a problem that we are not even aware exists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: A change of this magnitude to how community boards function should never be made without the boards themselves at the table, and we were not consulted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Manhattan Community Board 6 urges the committee to reject Intro 501 outright and to work in partnership with community boards to strengthen, not weaken, the systems that support good government at the neighborhood level.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Who's next?

chapters.summary: Dante Arnwine, district manager for Brooklyn CB9, shares that his board went five to six years without a district manager and it took five years to build community trust. He asks council members to meet their district managers and support budget increases for small operational needs.
chapters.title: Arnwine CB9 Brooklyn on trust and community service

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Good morning, Dante Arnwine, District Manager, Brooklyn Community Board 9.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: First, I just want to take the opportunity to thank my colleagues for their strong testimony so far.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: A little bit about this is trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: My story as district manager is a little different than most.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Prior to being district manager, our board at CB9 didn't have a district manager for five to six years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: So what does that mean?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Think about if your district

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: didn't have a representative, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: That means that your community, all the community conversations, whether it's about sanitation, ULERT, transportation, all of those community conversations, that community gets left out of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And that's what I walked into when I took the job as district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And I can tell you after five years of being on the job, it's taken five years for me to gain the trust of not only board members who have come in and out of the board, but also the people that I serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: I know that because I can stand in a room now with people who used to sue

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: our community board, our elected officials, and now I can speak to them and have that cordial conversation about how we get things done together to move the needle on things that we might not necessarily agree on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And so I think that this position in itself is really a

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: about the trust of the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Elected officials come and go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: So do board members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: But typically, district managers are there for some time serving.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: There's no better feeling I get when I get a constituent who comes into the office and says, I voted for you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And I get to say, no, you didn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: This is not an elected position.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And so no matter who the person is, no matter what they think, their political party, we get to serve in a nonpartisan, in a fair manner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And so I ask that the city council, for the members who don't know their district managers,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Reach out to your district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Ask them what they need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Ask them what they're going through.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Because I can tell you from my time as district manager, I've done it all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And we're asking for support from the city council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And it's for various things, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Wild things happen in a community board office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: One date.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: The gate goes out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: That's $10,000, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: That literally just happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And so these are small little things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: We can't call and find a vendor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: These are small things we need help with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: So I ask the council to oblige all the requests to increase our budgets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: And again, if you don't know your district manager, meet with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dante Arnwine
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Shawn Campbell, district manager for Brooklyn CB14, opposes Intro 501 and reports losing a staff member due to inability to offer merit increases. She suggests pegging a baseline budget increase to a formula, noting the $50,000 figure was derived from borough president budget increases and full staffing needs.
chapters.title: Campbell CB14 Brooklyn on budget and 501

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: Hello, Shawn Elise Campbell, District Manager, Brooklyn Community Board 14.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: I would just reiterate what my colleagues and their chairs have said today, and I would just reiterate the four previous times I've come to testify before this body.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: And in fact, to save money, I found some hard copies the last time I testified, so I've just updated the date and resubmitted that testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: One thing that has changed since submitting that testimony back in March is that I had at that time two community coordinators plus myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: I now have one because we are at the point in our budgets at CB14 where we will not be able to offer, well, now that there's a staffing change, this has changed, but we were not able to offer a merit increase to anybody, not even a percentage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: We...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: So I have a staffer who left for a job where salary increases are on her horizon again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: I did want to speak to the question about how much, because the idea of at least $50,000 for a baseline budget increase, that figure derived from the idea that we wanted

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: an annual budget increase to be tied to something so that we didn't have to come begging annually since it doesn't clearly work out for us very often.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: And the $50,000 was pegged to the borough president's budget increases over the years and or a full complement of staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: That's what would have made us whole a couple of years ago when we came up with that number.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: So that number would have to increase, and then I would encourage the council to consider pegging a baseline budget increase for community boards to a formula or to another pegged budget increase for...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: Future ability to plan and continue to serve our communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Carl Henry Cesar, former chair of Brooklyn CB14, calls Intro 501 a reckless power grab, comparing it to elected officials firing staff at other agencies. He argues that with member term limits approaching, borough presidents should focus on picking better members, not gaining firing power over district managers.
chapters.title: Cesar CB14 Brooklyn opposes 501 as power grab

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Chairwoman Brewer and members of the committee, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Thank you very much for allowing me to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: So my name is Carl Henry Cesar.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: I am a current board member of Brooklyn Community Board 14.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: I am the former chairperson of Brooklyn Community Board 14, and I am testifying on my behalf.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Again, I'm not testifying on behalf of the board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: And so I'm here in opposition to 501 to restate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: what everyone said so eloquently before me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: I think, number one, it is a reckless power grab.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: I think by 501's standpoint, then Councilmember Buehr, you could fire a legislative aide from Councilmember Morano's office, and then the mayor could fire someone from a legislative aide's office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: There's no end to that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Number two, I think it's operationally abusive because, as people have said before me, which is very eloquent, term limits are coming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: So that puts more stress on the district offices, which puts more stress on the borough president's offices to pick the right people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: So I feel like given that forthcoming climate, it makes more sense and better use of their time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: To figure out how to pick better people, not figure out, oh, now I can fire district managers as well, too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: And then the third thing is because I'm a fan of sports, there are many franchises across many sports that languish for decades because their management medals in the wrong things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: The Cowboys have languished for decades because Jerry Jones medals in the wrong things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Dan Snyder with the Washington Commanders meddled in the wrong things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: If this bill passes, you will have borough presidents meddling in the wrong things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: And so that's it for me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Amal Micah, director of visual design from the Staten Island Borough President's office, presents the design process for the Staten Island flag, showing pencil sketches and the journey to the final design, which received press coverage in the Staten Island Advance and the New York Times.
chapters.title: Micah on Staten Island flag design and symbolism

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Brewer and committee members, and particularly Frank Morano for inviting me here to talk about this favorite project,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: that being the design of the flag for Staten Island, which is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: I promise it wasn't created with artificial intelligence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: It replaced this one, which then Borough President James Otto brought to me and said, we've got to redo this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We're being represented poorly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And so happily, we got started on a brand new flag.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We began with the basics.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: There was some equity built into the Staten Island seal, which existed, but as far as I was concerned, it looked like it was squeezed out of a toothpaste tube.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: So it needed some freshening and some smartening up and crisp graphics.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We began pencil sketches.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We had our colleagues pose for the illustrator.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: It's Michelle Kakoza, my partner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And then we took the step-by-step journey to a final design, which was this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And so.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We got a little bit of press.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We got a story in the Saturday Night Advance and a full page in the New York Times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: So we were quite pleased.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And thank you again for allowing me to speak about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I like it a lot.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then I learned from the briefing paper, thank you, Council Member Brown, that Manhattan doesn't have it codified either.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was very interesting.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I saw other boroughs do, so like Staten Island and Manhattan, they're not.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You've got great leadership with Frank Morano, and Jimmy Otto is my hero.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: He's a lot of folks'heroes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So anybody, you have questions?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Morano asks Micah why a flag matters and about potentially controversial symbolism. Micah explains the flag's allegorical content: Henry Hudson's ship the Half Moon, indigenous people greeting him, a figure representing the city with a downward-pointing sword indicating peace, and turtle doves that mate for life on the shield.
chapters.title: Morano questions Micah on flag meaning and symbolism

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I have one question for Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Micah on the flag and then one question for the community board panel.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Micah, one, thank you not only for being here today and spending hours here and waiting to testify and give some of the perspective and the history of the flag.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I have a two-part question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One is...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What does it matter what flag a borough has?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Staten Islanders still have to pay the same taxes.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They still have to deal with the same problems with cost of living and crime and sanitation and everything.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Who cares what kind of flag that we have?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And second, a lot of my colleagues in the council, because of concerns about how things used to be in the 17th century, 18th century, 19th century,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: 18th century and evolving trends of treatment of American Indian groups, for instance, and numerous other groups that were formerly mistreated by European colonial settlers.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They wonder what actually is in this flag and is there somebody or something in this flag that might be controversial.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'm wondering if you can actually explain some of the symbolism, some of the images in the flag itself so my colleagues know what they're voting on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: We liked very much the original seal for its allegorical content.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And that is to say, Henry Hudson came into the Narrows in 1609.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: So his ship, the Half Moon, is portrayed there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: He was greeted by indigenous people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: Staten Islanders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: It wasn't Staten Island yet, but they were farmers and fishermen who greeted him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: This figure, again, an allegorical figure, represents the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: Her sword is pointed down, indicating peace.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: Her shield contains two turtle doves who mate for life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: So we thought there was great value in the imagery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: It just needed to be brought into contemporary feeling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And so that...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And to what you asked for, I've been privileged to work as an art director and graphic designer all my life, most recently for the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: It has always been my goal in doing my work to communicate ideas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And for Staten Island to be proud of a flag that represents us, I think, has value.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Micah
utterances.text: And so if we can bring it up to a level that somebody can look at and say, yeah, I feel good about that, then I think that was the purpose.

chapters.summary: Morano asks the community board panel for opinions on giving council members binding appointment power over half of community board members. McKee supports the current system, Cesar says it could work with good faith engagement from both parties, and Campbell notes a gap when council members fail to forward nominations.
chapters.title: Morano questions CB panel on Intro 322 appointments

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: My one question on the community board front for anybody that wants to answer it is, what do you think of the idea of allowing council members to appoint half the community board members in a binding rather than an advisory manner?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: For anybody.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Yeah, just turn your mic on if you do want to comment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: I was going to say, I'm the chair of my local community board, and it's a really robust review that happens now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: And the community board actually has the opportunity to opine, to say, oh, you know, this person has come to meetings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: They're interested in the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: They volunteer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: And especially for new council members, that's just not available.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: So I really think it should be the current system that we have where the borough president works with the council members to determine from the pool of people that have applied who would be the best fit for that community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy McKee
utterances.text: Our board has not taken a stand on this, so this is my personal opinion at this point.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Anybody else have a personal opinion they want to share?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Well, of the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Yes, so personal opinion, I agree with what the chairperson said, where

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Yes, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Yeah, just to reiterate, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: So yes, I agree with that, where it would have to be

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Like, basically, the two things that would have to work would be a borough president's office that would be equally engaging with that city council member, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Just because if one of those two weren't participating in, like, full faith, then that would fall apart.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: I don't, because my concerns would be...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: Not only some of the politicization, but also just the fact that you would need both parties to see, hey, who's saying lip service that they're coming to the community board meetings and engaging versus who's actually doing so.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: So I think that partnership, I think if both parties are engaging in good faith with each other, then yes, I'm a fan of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carl Henry Cesar
utterances.text: But I do have hesitation just outright.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: Last time we were together and spoke about this, I said that in Brooklyn it didn't feel like it would have a practical

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: impact because the borough presidents that I've been district manager while they've been BPs, and there's three of them, have always honored the nominations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: And the only time where there was a glitch in that system was when the council members did not turn in names for their nominations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: And then whose responsibility was it to fill those slots?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: Some borough presidents argued that without nominations, those were going to be vacancies, and others understood the language of the charter to direct them because it says borough presidents shall and I think council members may.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: So I think that's where the glitch could continue, even if this were to pass, is what happens if names aren't forwarded by council members?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: So I would, if the council does consider this, I would look at that potential gap.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: And there's a theme today where there's some things already written into the language of the charter and the law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shawn Elise Campbell
utterances.text: And if it's not being enforced now, and instead we're just introducing new laws on top of the ones that aren't being enforced, then there's a systems problem that should also be considered.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right on my, okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you everybody, you're fabulous.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I love community boards, I love district managers.

chapters.summary: Brewer transitions to virtual testimony, instructing panelists to wait for the sergeant to announce they may begin before delivering testimony.
chapters.title: Brewer transitions to virtual panelists

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Now we will turn to the virtual panelists.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: For virtual panelists, once your name is called, a member of our staff will unmute you, and the sergeant at arms will set the timer and give you the go-ahead to begin.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Please wait for the sergeant to announce that you may begin before delivering your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And now I have several names.

chapters.summary: Wayne Rosenfeld, former 10-year member of Staten Island CB3, supports Intro 322, arguing no single elected official should control all community board appointments in a borough. He says shared appointments between borough presidents and council members create balance, accountability, and broader representation.
chapters.title: Rosenfeld supports Intro 322 - shared appointments

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: First, we're going to Wayne Rosenfeld.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair and members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: I'm here in support of Intro 0322.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: I am a former 10-year member of Community Board 3 on Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Professionally, I am the executive

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Executive Director of the East Midwood Jewish Center in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: When I first joined community board, appointments were made through cooperation between elected officials.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Recommendations came from different voices, different perspectives, and different parts of the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: The result was a board that was better reflected for people it served.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Today, community board members are ultimately appointed by a single borough president.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: While the borough presidents play an important role, no elected official should have the exclusive control over the composition of every community board in an entire borough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Community boards work best when they reflect the full range of voices within a community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: No single elected official, regardless of party or philosophy, should be able to shape the membership of an entire community board on their own.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Community boards are intended to represent neighborhoods, not the agenda of any one officeholder.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: A shared appointment process promotes independence, diversity of thought, and better outcomes for the communities these boards serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Council members are elected directly by the communities they represent and often have the closest connection to the neighborhood concerns and local stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Given council members the authority to appoint half of the members creates balance, accountability, and broader representation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: New York City has 59 community districts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Manhattan is 12, Brooklyn is 18, Queens is 14, the Bronx is 12, and Staten Island only three.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Each community has its own character, priorities, and challenges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: It is unreasonable to expect five borough presidents

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: alone to determine the membership of all 59 boards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: This bill does not eliminate the borough president's role.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: It simply creates a shared appointment process, ensuring that more voices are involved and the community boards better reflect the communities they serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: For those reasons, I urge the council to support Intro to 322, and thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Clifford Hazen.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: He waited for two hours, but I had to go back to his job as a teacher.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Eddie Mark?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Eddie Mark?

chapters.summary: Eddie Mark, district manager for Brooklyn CB13, opposes Intro 501, arguing four-year terms are insufficient time to grasp community needs and that allowing elected officials to fire staff of an independent city agency is without precedent and against city charter regulations.
chapters.title: Eddie Mark CB13 Brooklyn opposes 501

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Rosenfeld
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: My name is Eddie Mark.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: I am the district manager for CB13 down here in Coney Island, Brighton Beach, Seagate, and Gravesend.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: I'm going to read my testimony to the members here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: So to the members of the committee and New York City Council member at large, thank you for the opportunity to submit this testimony in reference to Intro

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: 501, a local law amend to the New York City Charter in relation to the term of the employment for district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: This bill will limit the term of community board district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: To four years with the possibility of reappointment and provide a borough president with the power to remove district managers at will.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: With this magnitude of projects and situation that takes place within a community board, it stands to reason that a four-year term isn't sufficient time for a district manager to grasp the needs and issues

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: of a diverse community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: Community boards are now and have always been independent, non-mayor city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: They are advisory bodies on proposed

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: permit, event, and applications and overriding responsibilities of a community is to be a mediator between the community and the city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: The community board independence must be preserved and the board's

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: function must be supported with a budget that works with inflation and with all city agencies and elected officials.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: The idea that an elected official, the mayor or the mayoral president, can fire the staff of an independent city agency for no reason is without precedent

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: and against all regulations of the city charter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: This proposal must be withdrawn entirely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eddie Mark
utterances.text: This is submitted by my chair, Jeff Sandel, and myself, Eddie Mark, as district.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The next is, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Sabrina Massey.

chapters.summary: Sabrina Massey, a new community board member at CB16, opposes term limits for district managers, arguing it would limit information available to board members, discourage people from becoming district managers or board members, and disrupt the relationship between them.
chapters.title: Massey CB16 opposes DM term limits

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: Hi, can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: Thank you for having me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: My name is Sabrina Massey, and I'm here to say that I am against the term limits for district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: I'm with CB16.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: I'm a community board member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: And as a new community board member, CB16 have a lot of challenges and a lot of issues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: And if you put a term limit on the district manager, that's limiting the information that the board members are going to be able to receive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: We already have a challenge with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: Having the board members show up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: So with the work of the district manager who has to learn a wealth of information and pass it down to the board members, that's going to have a challenge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: The board members will also suffer with knowledge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: It's going to limit people wanting to become a board member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: As well as a district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: So I am against it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: I think that once the board members and the district managers form a relationship and try to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: have a successful board, that's going to be taken away if you put a term limit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: So thank you for letting me speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: And that is my opinion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sabrina Massey
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you for your service.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And Lisa Purdy is next.

chapters.summary: Annalisa Purdie, board member and legislative chair at CB16, opposes Intro 501, arguing it would limit community board decision-making power and replace institutional knowledge with disruption. She emphasizes the importance of stability and trust in neighborhood representation.
chapters.title: Purdie CB16 opposes 501

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: My name is Annalisa J.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: Purdy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: I am a board member also with Community Board 16.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: I work with Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: Massey in Ocean Hill, Brownsville, and I am the legislative chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: I'm also a fourth generation New Yorker, and I would like to reiterate the opposition to Intro 501, as has been mentioned by other community board members and district managers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: This proposed legislation would actually limit the power that community boards have to make decisions about their community

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: and would severely impact the work that is being done in the communities by replacing institutional knowledge and time spent both with

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: practical application of knowledge and the nuances that come with working with people within the community with the potential for disruption, the ramifications of which we would not be able to see for years to come.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: And in a time when community members across community board districts, across the five boroughs, are looking for stability in their neighborhoods and the opportunities to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: voice

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: their opinions in their neighborhoods and participate, the work of district managers is an integral part of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: They're not just colleagues, they are people who we have grown close to and who have a very intense knowledge of the different issues facing the community and that is knowledge that cannot be simply replaced or

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: constantly turned over by the disruptions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: And we also want to ensure that people are encouraged to serve on their community board and maintain, as is mentioned by previous community board members, that sense of trust that is integral to how community boards work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annalisa J. Purdie
utterances.text: Thank you for your time, and I will be submitting a written testimony as well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate that testimony.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Amanda Wilkinson?

chapters.summary: Giovanni Stigiano, board member at Brooklyn CB4, opposes Intro 501, arguing the district manager's institutional knowledge is critical especially as term-limited members depart. He warns the bill would cripple the board's ability to hold city agencies accountable in a neighborhood experiencing gentrification and nightlife growth.
chapters.title: Stigiano CB4 Brooklyn opposes 501

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: Good afternoon to the committee, Chair Brewer, and the rest of those that have testified today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: My name is Giovanni Stigiano, and I am a community board member with Brooklyn Community Board 4.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: To amplify the sentiments of the other community boards that have testified today, in addition to my direct district manager, Celestina Leon, and our chairperson, Robert Camacho.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: I'm also lending testimony against Intro 501.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: Our district manager is extremely knowledgeable, has a ton of institutional knowledge that is valuable to our community board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: And unlike other community boards in that, particularly where waiting, they are, you know, waiting lists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: To join the community board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: That's not something that we really experienced in Brooklyn CD4.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: And that's something that's experienced in other Brooklyn community boards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: Because of the loss that we're going to be experiencing next year with our chairperson, who is now term limited,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: It would, like the passing of this bill would severely cripple our community board because

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: So I would hope that this bill also just stops here and it doesn't move on any further because, again, the processes exist to already remove a district manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: And if that happens in our Brooklyn Community Board 4, many of our knowledgeable members are also 10 million and leaving next year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: We won't have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: It's still experiencing hostility from landlords.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: It's having a tremendous growth in nightlife, concentrated in a single area, something that the state liberal authority, you know, honestly doesn't give.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: Two thoughts about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano
utterances.text: And our borough president is like up, you know, he might be in Congress, you know, like he's on his way out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Stigiano

summary: Oversight hearing on community board resources and structure, with testimony from COIB, CFB, CEC, and numerous community board district managers and members on five bills.

Bills discussed: Intro 12A (AI disclosure in elections), Intro 320 (Staten Island flag), Intro 322 (council member community board appointments), Intro 501A (district manager terms), and Intro 951 (prediction market insider trading).

Strong opposition from community board members, district managers, and borough presidents to Intro 501A imposing term limits on district managers, with advocates urging budget increases for community boards instead.
title: Committee on Governmental Operations, State & Federal Legislation
